---
title: "Final Exercise"
format: html
---

**End of day | ~45 minutes**

This exercise ties together everything from the course into one complete workflow.

---

## Part 1 — Plan on GitHub (~15 min)

### 1a. Create an Issue

1. Go to `https://github.com/DQBM-SIB/intro_git_github`
2. Click **Issues** → **New issue**
3. Title: `YOUR_NAME — project description`
4. Body: list 3–5 things you plan to add
5. Assign to yourself, add a label (e.g., `documentation`)
6. Submit — note your **issue number** (e.g., `#22`)

### 1b. Add to the Project board

1. On the issue page, find **Projects** in the right sidebar
2. Add to the course project board → move it to **In Progress**

---

## Part 2 — Work locally (~15 min)

### 2a. Clone the course repo

```bash
cd ~/Downloads
git clone git@github.com:DQBM-SIB/intro_git_github.git course_repo
cd course_repo
git log --oneline
```

### 2b. Create a branch

```bash
git checkout -b YOUR_FIRST_NAME
```

### 2c. Add your project description

```bash
nano projects.md
```

```markdown
# Your Full Name
Your project description here.
```

### 2d. Commit with your issue number

```bash
git add projects.md
git commit -m "added YOUR_NAME project description, closes #YOUR_ISSUE_NUMBER"
```

---

## Part 3 — Push and Pull Request (~10 min)

```bash
git push origin YOUR_FIRST_NAME
```

On GitHub:

1. Click **Compare & pull request**
2. Base: `main` ← Compare: `YOUR_FIRST_NAME`
3. Title: `Added YOUR_NAME project description`
4. Description: `Closes #YOUR_ISSUE_NUMBER`
5. **Create pull request**

---

## Part 4 — After all PRs are merged (~5 min)

```bash
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git log --oneline    # see everyone's contributions
```

---

## Final challenge — no peeking until you've tried

Imagine: it's three months from now. A collaborator asks "can you re-run
exactly the analysis that produced the numbers in Table 2?" You don't
remember which version of your script that was.

**Question:** Using only what you learned today, how would you find and
re-run that exact version?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Click to reveal one possible approach

1. `git log --oneline` to scan commit messages for something like
   "generated Table 2" or a date near submission
2. `git diff <hash1> <hash2>` to confirm what changed between candidate commits
3. `git checkout <hash>` to put your working directory back exactly as it
   was at that commit (detached HEAD — fine for read-only inspection)
4. Re-run your script from that exact state
5. `git checkout main` to return to the latest version when done

This is the entire reason version control exists for research: not just
backup, but **exact reproducibility** of any past state, on demand.
:::

---

## What you demonstrated today

| Skill | Module |
|-------|--------|
| Version control concepts | 1 |
| Markdown formatting | 1 |
| `init`, `add`, `commit`, `log`, `diff` | 2 |
| Branching, merging, conflict resolution | 3 |
| `.gitignore`, `git reset` | 3 |
| SSH authentication | 4 |
| `push`, `pull`, `clone`, `remote` | 4 |
| Issues and Projects | 4 |
| Fork and Pull Request | 4 |

---

## Next steps

- Use Git for your own scripts from day one
- Branch whenever you try something experimental
- Write meaningful commit messages — your future self will thank you
- Reference: [Cheat Sheet](../qmd/cheatsheet.qmd)
